docs.title: Ecology: Systems Thinking for a Changing Planet
docs.text: Welcome to our interactive intelligent textbook on Ecology! This textbook is designed for high school students (grades 9-12) who want to understand how natural systems work and how they are connected together. This course covers topics aligned with introductory college-level ecology and college placement Environmental Science frameworks -- from ecosystems and biodiversity to climate change and environmental policy -- with 81 hands-on simulations, an interactive learning graph, and a focus on systems thinking and scientific literacy throughout. This course is not associated with The College Board in any way and no endorsement from The College Board is implied.

docs.title: What You Will Learn
docs.text:
1. Foundations of Ecology -- ecosystems, species, energy, matter
  2. Ecosystems and Biomes -- terrestrial and aquatic biomes, food webs, trophic levels
  3. Energy Flow -- primary productivity, thermodynamics, trophic efficiency
  4. Biogeochemical Cycles -- carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, and water cycles
  5. Species Interactions -- predation, competition, mutualism, keystone species
  6. Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services -- succession, island biogeography, tolerance
  7. Population Ecology -- growth models, carrying capacity, demographics
  8. Earth Systems and Resources -- plate tectonics, soils, atmosphere, climate patterns
  9. Sustainability and Energy Resources -- fossil fuels, renewables, nuclear, EROI
  10. Land and Water Use -- agriculture, mining, urbanization, sustainable practices
  11. Atmospheric Pollution -- smog, acid rain, criteria pollutants, Clean Air Act
  12. Water and Land Pollution -- eutrophication, toxicology, waste management
  13. Systems Thinking -- feedback loops, leverage points, resilience, emergence
  14. Scientific Literacy -- peer review, logical fallacies, statistical reasoning
  15. Global Climate Change -- greenhouse effect, tipping points, policy responses
  16. Biodiversity Loss and Policy -- HIPPO framework, endangered species, conservation
  17. Evaluating Environmental Claims -- misinformation, greenwashing, fact-checking

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• 81 Interactive MicroSims -- hands-on simulations for every major concept using p5.js, vis-network, and Chart.js
  • Learning Graph -- visual map of 360+ course concepts and their dependencies
  • Bailey the Beaver -- your learning mascot guides you with humor, puns, and systems thinking connections
  • Bloom's Taxonomy Alignment -- objectives at all six cognitive levels (Remember through Create)
  • Media Literacy Training -- every chapter includes source-checking and misinformation detection skills
  • Chapter Quizzes -- self-assessment questions with collapsible answers

docs.title: How to Use This Book
docs.text:
Navigate using the sidebar on the left. Start with the Course Description for a full overview, then explore the Learning Graph to see how concepts connect before diving into chapters. Try the MicroSims to interact with ecological concepts hands-on.

Note

This course covers topics aligned with the AP Environmental Science framework but is not affiliated with, endorsed by, or approved by the College Board. AP and Advanced Placement are registered trademarks of the College Board.

docs.title: Welcome From Bailey the Beaver
docs.text: Hey there, builders! Welcome to the interactive intelligent textbook Ecology: Systems Thinking for a Changing Planet. My name is Bailey the Beaver, and I'll be your guide throughout this book. I'll introduce each chapter, highlight key connections, give you tips and encouragement, warn you about common misunderstanding, and help you see how everything's connected! Think like an ecologist!

docs.title: Why This Book
docs.text:
Ecology is everywhere — in the food you eat, the air you breathe, the water you drink, and the landscapes you see outside your window. Yet most people never learn the systems thinking tools that ecologists use to understand how the natural world actually works. The result? Misleading environmental claims go unchallenged, viral posts about climate and conservation spread unchecked, and well-meaning people support ideas that ignore fundamental ecological trade-offs.

This book exists because high school students deserve an engaging, evidence-based resource that builds genuine ecological thinking skills — not just memorizing definitions, but developing the ability to trace energy flows, identify feedback loops, evaluate environmental claims with data, and spot misinformation in social media and journalism.

Every chapter is built around interactive MicroSims that let you manipulate population models, explore biogeochemical cycles, build food webs, and see ecological principles come alive. The goal isn't just to teach you ecology — it's to give you a superpower you'll use for the rest of your life.

docs.title: How to Use This Book
docs.text:
This textbook is designed for self-paced study. Each chapter builds on previous material, so reading in order is recommended. The book includes several types of resources:

  • 17 Chapters covering ecosystems, biodiversity, populations, Earth systems, pollution, global change, systems thinking, and scientific literacy
  • Interactive MicroSims embedded in chapters — browser-based simulations you can manipulate to explore concepts like predator-prey dynamics, carbon cycles, feedback loops, and population growth
  • Quizzes at the end of each chapter to test your understanding
  • Annotated References for each chapter linking to Wikipedia articles and authoritative sources
  • Glossary with defined terms for every key concept
  • FAQ with common questions and answers
  • Learning Graph visualizing concept dependencies across the course
  • Search available from any page using the search bar in the top navigation

The Learning Graph visualizes how concepts connect across chapters. If you want to explore non-linearly or check prerequisites for a specific topic, start there.

docs.title: About the Author
docs.text:
Dan McCreary is a semi-retired AI researcher, solution architect, and educator who has spent more than three decades helping Fortune 100 organizations reason over massive datasets. At Optum he founded the Generative AI Center of Excellence and led the team that built one of the world's largest healthcare knowledge graphs — spanning over 25 billion vertices — to unify member, provider, and patient insights. Dan's deep background in knowledge representation and systems thinking underpins the precise learning graphs and intelligent textbook workflows used throughout this course.

He is the co-author of Making Sense of NoSQL (Manning Publications), the founding chair of the NoSQL Now! conference, and a frequent keynote speaker on semantic search, ontology strategy, and AI hardware. Beyond industry, Dan has mentored students as a STEM volunteer since 2014 and now applies the same rigor to building open educational resources. You can visit the Intelligent Textbooks Case Studies to see over 71 textbooks that Dan has created or co-created with other authors.

Selected Credentials

  • B.A. in Physics and Computer Science from Carleton College
  • M.S.E.E. from the University of Minnesota
  • MBA coursework at the University of St. Thomas
  • Patent holder in semantic search and ontology management techniques
  • Advocate for large-scale Enterprise Knowledge Graph adoption across healthcare and education
  • Long-time promoter of accessible, low-cost AI-powered learning experiences

docs.title: Ecology: An Interactive Intelligent Textbook
docs.text:
Course Title: Ecology: Systems Thinking for a Changing Planet

Target Audience: High school students (grades 9-12) with an interest in biology, environmental science, and understanding how natural systems work. No prior coursework in ecology is required, though a basic understanding of biology and chemistry is helpful.

Prerequisites: Basic biology (cell structure, genetics fundamentals) and introductory chemistry (atoms, molecules, chemical reactions). Comfort with basic algebra and graph interpretation.

Reading Level: Grade 10 (Flesch-Kincaid)

Disclaimer

This course is not affiliated with, endorsed by, or approved by the College Board or any AP program. While the content covers topics aligned with introductory college-level ecology and environmental science curricula, including topics found in the AP Environmental Science framework, this is an independent educational resource. "AP" and "Advanced Placement" are registered trademarks of the College Board.

docs.title: Course Overview
docs.text:
Ecology is the science of connections. Every organism on Earth exists within a web of relationships -- with other species, with the physical environment, and with global systems that cycle matter and energy across the planet. This course takes a systems thinking approach to ecology, helping you see not just individual parts of the natural world but the patterns, feedback loops, and emergent behaviors that arise when those parts interact.

This interactive intelligent textbook goes beyond memorizing facts about ecosystems. You will learn to think like an ecologist -- building mental models of complex systems, evaluating evidence, designing investigations, and making predictions about how changes ripple through interconnected networks. These skills are not just useful in science class; they are essential tools for navigating a world where environmental claims fill your social media feeds, political debates, and daily news.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

  1. Explain how energy flows and matter cycles through ecosystems at multiple scales
  2. Analyze population dynamics using mathematical models and real-world data
  3. Evaluate how human activities alter Earth's systems and propose evidence-based solutions
  4. Apply systems thinking to identify feedback loops, leverage points, and unintended consequences in ecological and human systems
  5. Distinguish between credible scientific evidence and misinformation about environmental topics
  6. Design investigations to test ecological hypotheses using appropriate methods and controls
  7. Interpret quantitative data including graphs, statistical summaries, and mathematical models
  8. Communicate ecological concepts and evidence-based arguments clearly and precisely

docs.title: Unit 1: The Living World -- Ecosystems
docs.text:
Introduction to ecosystems and the fundamental processes that sustain them. Students explore terrestrial biomes (tropical rainforest, temperate forest, desert, tundra, grassland, chaparral, taiga) and aquatic biomes (freshwater streams, rivers, lakes, wetlands, estuaries, open ocean, coral reefs, deep ocean). Core biogeochemical cycles are examined in depth: the carbon cycle, nitrogen cycle, phosphorus cycle, and hydrologic (water) cycle. Students learn to calculate and interpret primary productivity (gross primary productivity vs. net primary productivity), trace energy through trophic levels using the 10% rule, and analyze food chains and food webs.

Systems Thinking Focus: Mapping feedback loops in biogeochemical cycles; understanding how a disruption in one cycle cascades through others.

docs.title: Unit 2: The Living World -- Biodiversity
docs.text:
Exploration of biodiversity at three levels -- genetic, species, and ecosystem diversity -- and why each matters. Students examine ecosystem services (provisioning, regulating, cultural, and supporting), island biogeography and species-area relationships, ecological tolerance ranges, natural disruptions to ecosystems (fires, floods, volcanic eruptions), adaptations, and ecological succession (primary and secondary). Keystone species, indicator species, foundation species, and invasive species are analyzed through case studies.

Systems Thinking Focus: How the loss of a single keystone species can restructure an entire ecosystem; modeling cascading effects.

docs.title: Unit 3: Populations
docs.text:
Population ecology concepts including generalist vs. specialist species, r-selected and K-selected reproductive strategies, and survivorship curves (Types I, II, III). Students work with mathematical models of population growth (exponential and logistic), carrying capacity, overshoot, and density-dependent and density-independent limiting factors. Human population dynamics are examined through age structure diagrams, total fertility rate, the Rule of 70, and the demographic transition model.

Systems Thinking Focus: Why exponential growth cannot continue indefinitely in a finite system; identifying delays and feedback in population regulation.

Critical Thinking Focus: Evaluating claims about human overpopulation and underpopulation -- who is making the claim, what data do they use, and what do they leave out?

docs.title: Unit 4: Earth Systems and Resources
docs.text:
The physical foundations of ecology: plate tectonics and how geological processes shape habitats over deep time, soil formation and erosion, soil composition and properties (soil horizons, soil texture triangle), Earth's atmosphere (layers and composition), global wind patterns and the Coriolis effect, watersheds, solar radiation and seasons, Earth's geography and climate patterns, and El Nino/La Nina (ENSO) cycles.

Systems Thinking Focus: How atmosphere, hydrosphere, lithosphere, and biosphere interact as coupled systems; tracing cause-and-effect across Earth system boundaries.

docs.title: Unit 5: Land and Water Use
docs.text:
Human use of land and water resources and the ecological consequences. Topics include the tragedy of the commons, clearcutting and deforestation, the Green Revolution, impacts of agricultural practices (monoculture, salinization, waterlogging, aquifer depletion), irrigation methods, pest control methods, meat production and CAFOs, overfishing and bycatch, mining impacts (surface and subsurface), urbanization and impervious surfaces, and ecological footprints. Sustainability solutions are explored: integrated pest management (IPM), sustainable agriculture (polyculture, crop rotation, no-till), aquaculture, sustainable forestry, and methods to reduce urban runoff.

Systems Thinking Focus: Mapping the unintended consequences of agricultural intensification; understanding why "solving" one problem (e.g., pest control with DDT) can create worse problems downstream.

Critical Thinking Focus: Analyzing food labeling claims ("organic," "natural," "sustainable") -- what do these terms actually mean, and what evidence supports or contradicts the marketing?

docs.title: Unit 6: Energy Resources and Consumption
docs.text:
Comprehensive examination of energy sources and their ecological impacts. Topics include renewable vs. nonrenewable resources, global energy consumption patterns, fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas -- formation, extraction, and environmental costs), nuclear power (fission, fusion, half-life, waste), biomass energy, solar energy (active, passive, photovoltaic), hydroelectric power, geothermal energy, hydrogen fuel cells, and wind energy. Energy conservation strategies, cogeneration, and energy return on investment (EROI) are analyzed.

Systems Thinking Focus: Tracing the full lifecycle of energy sources from extraction through waste; understanding why there is no perfectly "clean" energy and every choice involves trade-offs.

Critical Thinking Focus: Evaluating viral claims about renewable energy (e.g., "solar panels create more pollution than they prevent," "wind turbines kill millions of birds") -- checking sources, understanding scale and context, and spotting cherry-picked statistics.

docs.title: Unit 7: Atmospheric Pollution
docs.text:
Sources, effects, and reduction of air pollution. Topics include primary vs. secondary pollutants, the six criteria air pollutants (CO, SO2, NOx, O3, PM, Pb), volatile organic compounds (VOCs), photochemical smog formation, thermal inversions, atmospheric CO2 and particulates, indoor air pollutants (radon, asbestos, CO, mold), pollution reduction technologies (scrubbers, catalytic converters, electrostatic precipitators), acid rain (causes, effects, and policy responses), noise pollution, and the Clean Air Act.

Systems Thinking Focus: How local air pollution becomes a regional and global problem; feedback between pollution, health, economics, and policy.

docs.title: Unit 8: Aquatic and Terrestrial Pollution
docs.text:
Water and land pollution and their effects on ecosystems and human health. Topics include point vs. nonpoint source pollution, endocrine disruptors, human impacts on wetlands and mangroves, eutrophication (BOD, dissolved oxygen, algal blooms, dead zones), thermal pollution, persistent organic pollutants (POPs), bioaccumulation and biomagnification, solid waste disposal (landfills, incineration, recycling), waste reduction methods, sewage treatment (primary, secondary, tertiary), toxicology (LD50, dose-response curves, threshold effects), pollution and human health, pathogens and infectious diseases, and relevant legislation (Clean Water Act, CERCLA/Superfund).

Systems Thinking Focus: Tracing pollutants from source through bioaccumulation to apex predators and human food supplies; understanding time delays between pollution and observable effects.

Critical Thinking Focus: Evaluating claims about water quality, microplastics, and chemical safety -- understanding dose-response relationships and why "the dose makes the poison."

docs.title: Unit 9: Global Change
docs.text:
The largest-scale environmental challenges. Topics include stratospheric ozone depletion (CFCs, the ozone hole, the Montreal Protocol), the greenhouse effect, increases in greenhouse gases (CO2, CH4, N2O, water vapor), global climate change (evidence, models, projections), ocean warming, ocean acidification, invasive species, endangered species, human impacts on biodiversity, and the HIPPO framework (Habitat loss, Invasive species, Population growth, Pollution, Overexploitation). Policy frameworks are examined: the Kyoto Protocol, Paris Agreement, CITES, and the Endangered Species Act.

Systems Thinking Focus: Positive feedback loops in climate change (ice-albedo feedback, permafrost methane release); understanding tipping points and nonlinear change in global systems.

Critical Thinking Focus: Analyzing climate change communication -- distinguishing peer-reviewed science from opinion, identifying logical fallacies in climate debates, and understanding why scientific uncertainty does not mean scientific ignorance.

docs.title: Unit 10: Systems Thinking and Ecological Reasoning
docs.text:
A dedicated unit on the thinking tools that ecologists use. Topics include:

  • Stock and flow diagrams for modeling resource accumulation and depletion
  • Causal loop diagrams for identifying reinforcing and balancing feedback loops
  • Leverage points -- where small interventions can produce large systemic change
  • Emergence -- how complex behaviors arise from simple rules
  • Resilience and regime shifts -- why ecosystems can absorb some shocks but suddenly collapse under others
  • Scale and hierarchy -- how ecological patterns change depending on whether you zoom in or zoom out
  • Mental models -- recognizing your own assumptions and how they shape your conclusions

Systems Thinking Focus: This entire unit develops systems thinking as a transferable skill, with practice problems drawn from ecology, economics, public health, and everyday life.

docs.title: Unit 11: Evaluating Environmental Claims -- A Guide to Scientific Literacy
docs.text:
A dedicated unit on recognizing misinformation and building the skills to evaluate environmental claims encountered in social media, news, and everyday conversation. Topics include:

  • How science works -- the difference between hypotheses, theories, and laws; peer review; replication; scientific consensus
  • Common logical fallacies in environmental debates (false dichotomy, appeal to nature, cherry-picking, anecdotal evidence, correlation vs. causation)
  • Source evaluation -- identifying credible sources, checking funding and conflicts of interest, understanding the difference between primary research and media coverage
  • Statistical literacy -- understanding sample size, confidence intervals, margins of error, and why a single study rarely settles a question
  • Media literacy -- how headlines distort findings, how algorithms amplify outrage, and how to read beyond the headline
  • Case studies in misinformation -- examining real-world examples of environmental misinformation (climate denial, pesticide safety debates, species extinction claims, greenwashing) and practicing how to fact-check them
  • Constructive skepticism -- how to question claims without falling into cynicism; the difference between healthy skepticism and conspiratorial thinking

Critical Thinking Focus: This entire unit is devoted to building the skills students need to navigate a world flooded with competing environmental claims.

docs.title: Pedagogical Approach
docs.text:
This course uses an interactive intelligent textbook format with:

  • MicroSims -- interactive simulations built with p5.js that let students explore ecological concepts by changing parameters and observing outcomes (e.g., adjusting predator-prey ratios, modeling nutrient cycles, simulating climate feedback loops)
  • Concept dependency learning graphs -- visual maps showing how concepts build on each other, helping students identify prerequisites and plan their learning path
  • Glossary with precise definitions -- every technical term is defined concisely, distinctly, and without circular references
  • Multiple-choice quizzes aligned to Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (remember, understand, apply, analyze, evaluate, create)
  • Free-response practice -- investigation design, environmental problem analysis, and calculation-based questions
  • Real-world data sets -- students work with authentic ecological data, not just textbook examples

docs.title: Bloom's Taxonomy Integration
docs.text:
Course content and assessments span all six levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

Level Description Example Activity Remember Recall facts and basic concepts Define bioaccumulation; list the six criteria air pollutants Understand Explain ideas and concepts Describe how energy flows through trophic levels Apply Use information in new situations Calculate population doubling time using the Rule of 70 Analyze Draw connections among ideas Compare the ecological footprints of different diets Evaluate Justify a position or decision Assess whether a viral social media claim about deforestation is supported by evidence Create Produce new or original work Design an investigation to measure the effect of fertilizer runoff on aquatic dissolved oxygen

docs.title: Assessment Strategy
docs.text:
• Formative assessments: Interactive quizzes after each section with immediate feedback
  • MicroSim explorations: Guided and open-ended simulation activities with reflection questions
  • Data analysis exercises: Interpreting graphs, calculating rates, and drawing evidence-based conclusions
  • Misinformation analysis tasks: Students evaluate real social media posts and news articles about environmental topics
  • Systems mapping projects: Students create causal loop diagrams and stock-flow models for ecological systems
  • Summative assessments: End-of-unit tests combining multiple-choice and free-response questions

docs.title: Topics Covered (Summary)
docs.text: Unit Title Key Themes 1 The Living World -- Ecosystems Biomes, biogeochemical cycles, energy flow, trophic levels 2 The Living World -- Biodiversity Ecosystem services, island biogeography, succession 3 Populations Growth models, carrying capacity, human demographics 4 Earth Systems and Resources Plate tectonics, soil, atmosphere, climate patterns 5 Land and Water Use Agriculture, mining, urbanization, sustainability 6 Energy Resources and Consumption Fossil fuels, renewables, nuclear, conservation 7 Atmospheric Pollution Smog, acid rain, indoor pollution, Clean Air Act 8 Aquatic and Terrestrial Pollution Water pollution, toxicology, waste management 9 Global Change Climate change, ozone, biodiversity loss, policy 10 Systems Thinking Feedback loops, leverage points, resilience, mental models 11 Evaluating Environmental Claims Misinformation, source evaluation, statistical literacy

docs.title: Big Ideas
docs.text:
Four overarching themes run through every unit:

  1. Energy Transfer -- Energy transformations drive all ecological processes; energy is lost at each trophic step
  2. Interactions Between Earth Systems -- Earth comprises interconnected biogeochemical systems that create a stable ecosphere
  3. Interactions Between Species and Environment -- Organisms interact with each other and with the physical environment in ways that shape communities and ecosystems
  4. Sustainability -- Human activities alter natural systems; understanding these impacts is essential for making informed decisions about the future

docs.title: Science Practices
docs.text:
Students develop seven core science practices throughout the course:

  1. Concept Explanation -- Articulate ecological principles clearly and accurately
  2. Visual and Quantitative Representation -- Create and interpret diagrams, graphs, and models
  3. Text Analysis -- Extract and evaluate information from scientific texts and media reports
  4. Scientific Investigation Design -- Plan controlled experiments and field studies
  5. Data Analysis -- Interpret results using appropriate statistical and graphical methods
  6. Mathematical Routines -- Perform calculations relevant to ecology (growth rates, energy transfer, resource consumption)
  7. Environmental Solutions -- Propose and evaluate evidence-based solutions to environmental problems

docs.title: Course Duration
docs.text: Approximately 36 weeks (one academic year) with 5 hours of instruction per week. The course is designed to be self-paced, allowing students to spend more time on challenging topics and move quickly through familiar material.

docs.title: Learning Objectives by Bloom's Taxonomy Level
docs.text: The following learning objectives are organized according to the 2001 revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Anderson and Krathwohl). Each level builds on the levels below it, progressing from foundational knowledge to higher-order thinking.

docs.title: Remember
docs.text:
Students will be able to:

  1. List the six criteria air pollutants regulated by the Clean Air Act
  2. Name the four major biogeochemical cycles (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus, water)
  3. Identify the layers of Earth's atmosphere and soil horizons (O-A-B-C-R)
  4. Define key ecological terms including carrying capacity, trophic level, bioaccumulation, and ecological succession
  5. Recall the three levels of biodiversity (genetic, species, ecosystem)
  6. State the 10% rule of energy transfer between trophic levels
  7. List the greenhouse gases (CO2, CH4, N2O, water vapor) and their primary sources
  8. Identify the components of the HIPPO framework for biodiversity threats
  9. Name the three types of survivorship curves and the reproductive strategies they represent
  10. Recall the stages of the demographic transition model

docs.title: Understand
docs.text:
Students will be able to:

  1. Explain how energy flows through food webs and why energy is lost at each trophic level
  2. Describe the difference between exponential and logistic population growth
  3. Summarize how plate tectonics shapes habitats and influences species distribution over geological time
  4. Explain the difference between primary and secondary ecological succession
  5. Describe how the greenhouse effect works and why increased greenhouse gas concentrations lead to warming
  6. Distinguish between point source and nonpoint source pollution with examples of each
  7. Explain why bioaccumulation and biomagnification concentrate toxins in apex predators
  8. Describe how El Nino and La Nina events disrupt global weather patterns
  9. Summarize the tragedy of the commons and explain why shared resources are vulnerable to overexploitation
  10. Explain the difference between r-selected and K-selected reproductive strategies and the environments that favor each

docs.title: Apply
docs.text:
Students will be able to:

  1. Calculate population doubling time using the Rule of 70
  2. Use age structure diagrams to predict future population growth trends for a country
  3. Determine net primary productivity from gross primary productivity and respiration data
  4. Apply the species-area relationship to predict how habitat loss affects biodiversity
  5. Calculate ecological footprints and compare resource consumption across different lifestyles
  6. Read and interpret dose-response curves to determine LD50 values
  7. Use soil texture triangles to classify soil samples by composition
  8. Construct and interpret food webs from field observation data
  9. Apply the concept of carrying capacity to predict the outcome of resource limitation on a population
  10. Use causal loop diagrams to represent feedback relationships in a given ecological scenario

docs.title: Analyze
docs.text:
Students will be able to:

  1. Compare the ecological costs and benefits of different energy sources using EROI and lifecycle analysis
  2. Trace a pollutant from its source through bioaccumulation pathways to its effects on human health
  3. Analyze age structure diagrams to explain differences in population growth rates between countries
  4. Distinguish between reinforcing and balancing feedback loops in climate and ecosystem models
  5. Examine how the removal of a keystone species restructures community interactions
  6. Analyze the trade-offs between agricultural productivity and environmental sustainability
  7. Compare the effectiveness of different pollution reduction technologies (scrubbers, catalytic converters, electrostatic precipitators)
  8. Differentiate between correlation and causation in ecological data sets
  9. Analyze how urbanization alters local watersheds, heat distribution, and species composition
  10. Break down a complex environmental problem into interacting subsystems using stock-and-flow diagrams

docs.title: Evaluate
docs.text:
Students will be able to:

  1. Assess the credibility of an environmental claim by examining the source, methodology, sample size, and potential conflicts of interest
  2. Judge whether a social media post about climate change is consistent with the scientific consensus
  3. Evaluate the strengths and limitations of a proposed environmental policy (e.g., carbon tax, cap-and-trade, species protection act)
  4. Critique an experimental design for investigating an ecological hypothesis, identifying confounding variables and methodological weaknesses
  5. Assess whether a news headline accurately represents the findings of the underlying scientific study
  6. Evaluate competing land-use proposals by weighing ecological, economic, and social trade-offs
  7. Judge the validity of arguments for and against nuclear energy as a climate change mitigation strategy
  8. Determine whether a product's environmental marketing claims (e.g., "eco-friendly," "carbon neutral") are supported by evidence
  9. Evaluate whether a given dataset is sufficient to support a causal claim about an environmental relationship
  10. Assess the resilience of an ecosystem by identifying its feedback mechanisms, diversity, and proximity to tipping points

docs.title: Create
docs.text:
Students will be able to:

  1. Design a controlled investigation to test the effect of a specific variable on an ecological process
  2. Construct causal loop diagrams and stock-and-flow models for novel ecological scenarios
  3. Develop an evidence-based argument for or against a specific environmental policy, citing data and addressing counterarguments
  4. Build a systems map showing how a local environmental issue connects to regional and global processes
  5. Create a monitoring plan to detect early warning signs of ecosystem regime shifts
  6. Propose an integrated pest management strategy for a specific agricultural scenario
  7. Design a public communication piece that accurately conveys a complex ecological finding to a general audience
  8. Formulate predictions about how an ecosystem will respond to a specified disturbance using systems thinking principles
  9. Develop a fact-checking workflow for evaluating environmental claims encountered in social media and news
  10. Synthesize data from multiple sources to construct an original argument about the sustainability of a specific human practice

docs.title: Topics Not Covered
docs.text: This section defines the boundaries of the course to guide the learning graph and prevent concept drift into topics that require mathematical preparation beyond Algebra 1. Students in this course are assumed to be comfortable with variables, linear equations, ratios, percentages, and basic graph interpretation. They are not assumed to have completed Algebra 2, Pre-Calculus, Calculus, or college-level statistics.

docs.title: Mathematical Boundaries
docs.text:
The following topics are excluded because they require calculus, differential equations, or advanced statistics:

  • Differential equation models of population dynamics -- Lotka-Volterra predator-prey equations, continuous-time logistic growth (dN/dt = rN(1 - N/K)), and other ODE-based ecological models. Students will learn the conceptual behavior of these models (exponential vs. logistic curves, predator-prey oscillations) without the calculus formalism.
  • Integral calculus applications -- calculating area under curves for cumulative resource consumption, total pollution load over time, or net ecosystem exchange. Students will estimate these values from graphs using visual approximation rather than integration.
  • Partial differential equations -- reaction-diffusion models of species dispersal, spatial population dynamics, and heat/mass transfer in environmental systems.
  • Matrix population models -- Leslie matrices and eigenvalue analysis for structured population projections. Students will use age structure diagrams qualitatively instead.
  • Advanced statistical methods -- regression analysis, ANOVA, chi-square tests, multivariate analysis, principal component analysis, and hypothesis testing with p-values. Students will interpret pre-computed statistical results but will not perform these calculations.
  • Information theory and entropy -- Shannon diversity index derivation and information-theoretic model selection (AIC/BIC). Students will use simplified diversity measures (species richness, relative abundance) instead.
  • Thermodynamic formalism -- formal entropy calculations, Gibbs free energy in biogeochemical reactions, and energy balance equations using calculus. Students will understand energy flow conceptually using the 10% rule and energy pyramids.
  • Optimization and game theory -- optimal foraging theory with calculus-based optimization, evolutionary stable strategies with payoff matrices, and cost-benefit analysis using marginal rates.

docs.title: Ecology Subdisciplines Beyond Scope
docs.text:
The following specialized ecology topics are excluded because they are typically covered at the university level and require either advanced math or extensive prerequisite coursework:

  • Molecular ecology and phylogenetics -- DNA barcoding techniques, coalescent theory, molecular clock analysis, and computational phylogenetics. Basic genetics concepts (adaptation, natural selection, genetic diversity) are covered.
  • Quantitative genetics in ecology -- heritability calculations, selection gradients, quantitative trait loci, and the breeder's equation. Natural selection and adaptation are covered conceptually.
  • Landscape ecology modeling -- GIS-based spatial analysis, metapopulation models with extinction-colonization dynamics, percolation theory, and fractal geometry of habitat patches. Students will understand habitat fragmentation conceptually.
  • Theoretical ecology -- bifurcation analysis, chaos theory in ecological systems, stochastic population models, and analytical stability analysis of equilibria. Students will explore these dynamics through interactive simulations rather than formal mathematical analysis.
  • Ecosystem modeling and simulation -- computational fluid dynamics for ocean/atmosphere modeling, process-based biogeochemical models (e.g., CENTURY, BIOME-BGC), and general circulation models. Students will interpret model outputs and understand model assumptions without building these models.
  • Environmental economics formalism -- discounting future costs with net present value calculations, econometric modeling of environmental externalities, and Pigouvian tax optimization. Students will understand the concepts of externalities, commons, and trade-offs without the economic math.
  • Ecotoxicology beyond LD50 -- pharmacokinetic modeling, compartment models for toxicant distribution, and probabilistic risk assessment. Students will interpret dose-response curves and understand bioaccumulation without the underlying pharmacological math.
  • Paleoclimatology and isotope analysis -- oxygen isotope ratio calculations, radiometric dating mathematics, and spectral analysis of climate proxy records. Students will learn about climate change evidence without the isotope chemistry.

docs.title: Other Exclusions
docs.text:
• Laboratory techniques -- this is a conceptual and simulation-based course, not a lab course. Wet lab protocols (water quality testing kits, soil sampling methodology, specimen collection and preservation) are described but not performed.
  • Field research methodology -- mark-recapture statistics (Lincoln-Petersen method calculations), quadrat sampling with statistical analysis, and transect survey design with power analysis. Students will understand these methods conceptually and interpret example data.
  • Environmental law and policy details -- detailed legal analysis of specific regulations, case law, and regulatory compliance procedures. Students will understand major legislation (Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, ESA, CERCLA) at a conceptual level.
  • Engineering solutions -- detailed engineering design of water treatment plants, smokestack scrubber systems, solar panel circuitry, or nuclear reactor containment. Students will understand what these technologies do and their trade-offs without the engineering specifics.

docs.title: What is this textbook about?
docs.text: This is an interactive intelligent textbook called Ecology: Systems Thinking for a Changing Planet. It covers how ecosystems work, how energy and matter move through the natural world, how species interact, and how human activities affect the environment. What makes it different from a traditional textbook is its focus on systems thinking -- understanding how everything in nature is connected through feedback loops, cycles, and cascading effects. You will not just memorize facts about ecosystems; you will learn to think like an ecologist and apply those thinking skills to real-world environmental issues. The course spans 17 chapters organized into 11 units, covering everything from biomes and biogeochemical cycles to climate change, pollution, and how to evaluate environmental claims you encounter on social media. See the full Course Description for details.

docs.title: Who is this textbook designed for?
docs.text: This textbook is designed for high school students in grades 9 through 12 who are interested in biology, environmental science, or understanding how natural systems work. You do not need to have taken an ecology course before. The reading level targets grade 10 on the Flesch-Kincaid scale, which means the language is clear and accessible without being oversimplified. The course works well as a standalone ecology course, as a supplement to a biology or environmental science class, or as self-paced independent study for curious students who want to understand how the natural world works.

docs.title: What prerequisites do I need?
docs.text: You should have a basic understanding of biology (cell structure and genetics fundamentals) and introductory chemistry (atoms, molecules, and chemical reactions). You should also be comfortable with basic algebra and graph interpretation. You do not need calculus, advanced statistics, or any prior ecology coursework. The course is intentionally designed to stay within Algebra 1 math skills -- variables, linear equations, ratios, percentages, and reading graphs. If you can solve for x and read a bar chart, you have the math background you need.

docs.title: Is this an AP course?
docs.text: No. This course is not affiliated with, endorsed by, or approved by the College Board or any AP program. While the content covers many topics that overlap with the AP Environmental Science (APES) framework, this is an independent educational resource. The course takes its own approach, with a stronger emphasis on systems thinking, interactive simulations, and media literacy than a typical AP course. If you are preparing for the APES exam, this textbook can be a helpful supplementary resource, but it is not designed as an AP test prep tool.

docs.title: What are MicroSims and how do I use them?
docs.text: MicroSims are small interactive simulations built with p5.js (a JavaScript graphics library) that let you explore ecological concepts by changing parameters and watching what happens. For example, you might adjust predator and prey populations to see how they oscillate, change nutrient inputs to watch eutrophication develop, or tweak greenhouse gas levels to observe temperature changes. Each MicroSim runs directly in your web browser -- no downloads or special software needed. You interact with them using sliders, buttons, and dropdowns. The textbook includes 81 MicroSims across all chapters. Visit the MicroSims page for the full collection.

docs.title: What is the Learning Graph?
docs.text: The Learning Graph is a visual map showing how the 380 concepts in this course connect to and build on each other. Think of it as a dependency diagram: before you can truly understand biomagnification, for example, you need to understand bioaccumulation, food chains, and trophic levels. The Learning Graph makes these prerequisite relationships visible so you can plan your study path, identify gaps in your understanding, and see how individual topics fit into the bigger picture. You can explore it interactively at the Learning Graph page.

docs.title: Who is Bailey the Beaver?
docs.text: Bailey is the course mascot -- a friendly, industrious beaver who pops up throughout the textbook to offer tips, highlight key concepts, warn about common mistakes, and celebrate your progress. Bailey wears a green hard hat and has a warm, encouraging personality. Bailey's catchphrase is "Everything's connected!" which reflects the systems thinking focus of the course. You will see Bailey in colored callout boxes throughout the chapters. Bailey is not just decorative -- the mascot's appearances are timed to help you at specific moments: when a new topic is introduced, when a concept is especially tricky, or when you have completed a major section.

docs.title: How long does this course take to complete?
docs.text: The course is designed for approximately 36 weeks (one academic year) at about 5 hours of instruction per week. However, it is fully self-paced, so you can move faster through material you find easy and spend more time on challenging topics. Some students working independently might complete it in less time; others might spread it over a longer period. Each chapter includes reading, MicroSim explorations, quizzes, and practice problems. A reasonable estimate for a single chapter is one to three weeks depending on its complexity and how deeply you engage with the simulations and exercises.

docs.title: How should I use this textbook for the best learning experience?
docs.text: Start by reading the Course Description to understand the scope and goals. Then explore the Learning Graph to see how concepts connect. As you work through each chapter, do not skip the MicroSims -- interacting with simulations builds much deeper understanding than reading alone. Take the quizzes to check your comprehension, and pay attention to Bailey's callouts since they flag the most important concepts and common pitfalls. Use the Glossary whenever you encounter unfamiliar terms. If a concept feels confusing, check the Learning Graph to make sure you have a solid grasp of the prerequisite topics.

docs.title: Can I use this textbook on my phone or tablet?
docs.text: Yes. The textbook is built as a responsive website that works on desktops, laptops, tablets, and smartphones. The MicroSims are designed to run in any modern web browser with JavaScript enabled. For the best experience with simulations, a larger screen (tablet or laptop) is recommended since some MicroSims have multiple controls and displays that benefit from more screen space. But all reading content, quizzes, and the glossary work well on any device.

docs.title: How is this textbook different from a traditional ecology textbook?
docs.text: Three major differences set this textbook apart. First, the systems thinking emphasis means you learn to see connections, feedback loops, and cascading effects rather than studying topics in isolation. Second, the 81 interactive MicroSims let you experiment with ecological concepts hands-on instead of just reading about them. Third, the dedicated chapters on evaluating environmental claims and media literacy teach you to critically assess the environmental information you encounter every day on social media, in news articles, and in advertising. Traditional textbooks rarely address misinformation detection as a core skill.

docs.title: Is there a glossary I can reference?
docs.text: Yes. The course includes a comprehensive Glossary with 380 precisely defined terms. Every technical term used in the textbook has a clear, concise definition that avoids circular references (we never define a word using itself). The glossary is designed to be a quick-reference tool you can check any time you encounter unfamiliar terminology. Terms are organized alphabetically for easy lookup.

docs.title: What technology do I need to use this textbook?
docs.text: You need a device with a modern web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, or Edge) and an internet connection. JavaScript must be enabled for the MicroSims to run. No special software, plugins, or downloads are required. The textbook works on Windows, macOS, Linux, Chromebooks, iPads, and Android tablets. For the best experience, use a screen at least 10 inches wide so the MicroSim controls and displays are easy to interact with.

docs.title: How are the chapters organized?
docs.text: The 17 chapters are organized into 11 units that follow a logical progression. You start with foundational ecology concepts (ecosystems, biomes, energy flow, biogeochemical cycles), move into biodiversity and species interactions, explore population ecology and earth systems, examine human impacts (land use, energy, pollution), address global change (climate change, ozone depletion, biodiversity loss), and conclude with dedicated units on systems thinking tools and evaluating environmental claims. See Chapter 1 to begin.

docs.title: What is an ecosystem?
docs.text: An ecosystem is a community of living organisms (plants, animals, microbes) interacting with each other and with their nonliving physical environment (water, soil, air, sunlight, temperature). Ecosystems can be as small as a puddle or as large as the Amazon rainforest. What defines an ecosystem is not its size but the fact that energy flows and matter cycles within it through interconnected processes. Every ecosystem has biotic components (the living things) and abiotic components (the nonliving factors). Understanding ecosystems is the foundation of ecology because it frames how we think about all the relationships and processes that sustain life. See Chapter 1 for a full introduction.

docs.title: What is the difference between a biome and an ecosystem?
docs.text: A biome is a large-scale category of ecosystems that share similar climate conditions, vegetation types, and animal communities. Examples include tropical rainforest, tundra, desert, and coral reef. An ecosystem is a specific, localized community of organisms and their physical environment. So "temperate forest" is a biome, while the specific patch of forest behind your school is an ecosystem within that biome. A single biome contains thousands of individual ecosystems. Biomes are classified primarily by climate (temperature and precipitation patterns), while ecosystems are defined by the specific organisms and abiotic factors present in a particular place.

docs.title: How does energy flow through an ecosystem?
docs.text: Energy enters most ecosystems as sunlight, which producers (plants, algae, and some bacteria) capture through photosynthesis and convert into chemical energy stored in organic molecules. When a primary consumer (herbivore) eats a plant, it obtains some of that energy. When a secondary consumer (carnivore) eats the herbivore, energy transfers again. At each step, roughly 90% of the energy is lost as heat through cellular respiration, leaving only about 10% available to the next trophic level. This is called the 10% rule. It explains why food chains rarely have more than four or five levels and why there are far fewer top predators than plants in any ecosystem. Energy flow is a one-way process -- unlike matter, energy does not cycle.

docs.title: What are biogeochemical cycles and why do they matter?
docs.text: Biogeochemical cycles are the pathways by which chemical elements and compounds move between living organisms, the atmosphere, water, and the Earth's crust. The four major cycles covered in this course are the carbon cycle, nitrogen cycle, phosphorus cycle, and water (hydrologic) cycle. Unlike energy, which flows one way through ecosystems and is eventually lost as heat, matter is recycled. The carbon atoms in your body have been cycling through living things, the atmosphere, and rocks for billions of years. These cycles matter because disrupting them -- by burning fossil fuels (carbon cycle), over-applying fertilizer (nitrogen and phosphorus cycles), or diverting rivers (water cycle) -- can cause widespread environmental problems like climate change, eutrophication, and water scarcity.

docs.title: What is the carbon cycle?
docs.text: The carbon cycle traces how carbon atoms move between the atmosphere, living organisms, the ocean, and the Earth's crust. Key processes include photosynthesis (plants pull CO2 from the air and convert it to organic carbon), cellular respiration (organisms release CO2 back to the air), decomposition (dead organisms release carbon), ocean absorption (oceans dissolve CO2), and combustion (burning fossil fuels or biomass releases stored carbon). Over millions of years, carbon gets locked in fossil fuels and sedimentary rock. When humans burn fossil fuels, we release ancient carbon back into the atmosphere far faster than natural processes can absorb it, which is the primary driver of climate change.

docs.title: What is biodiversity and why does it matter?
docs.text: Biodiversity refers to the variety of life at three levels: genetic diversity (variation within a species), species diversity (the number and variety of species in an area), and ecosystem diversity (the range of different ecosystems in a region). Biodiversity matters because it provides ecosystem services that humans depend on -- provisioning services (food, medicine, materials), regulating services (pollination, water purification, climate regulation), cultural services (recreation, spiritual value), and supporting services (nutrient cycling, soil formation). More diverse ecosystems tend to be more resilient, meaning they can better withstand and recover from disturbances. When biodiversity declines, ecosystems lose functionality and become more vulnerable to collapse. See Chapter 2 for details.

docs.title: What is a keystone species?
docs.text: A keystone species is an organism that has a disproportionately large effect on its ecosystem relative to its abundance. Remove a keystone species and the entire community structure can change dramatically. The classic example is the sea otter in Pacific kelp forests: sea otters eat sea urchins, which eat kelp. When otters were hunted nearly to extinction, sea urchin populations exploded and devoured the kelp forests, destroying habitat for hundreds of other species. Keystone species are important because they demonstrate how a single species can hold an ecosystem together -- a powerful example of systems thinking in action.

docs.title: What is the difference between a food chain and a food web?
docs.text: A food chain is a simple, linear sequence showing how energy passes from one organism to the next (for example: grass, rabbit, fox, eagle). A food web is a more realistic representation showing all the interconnected food chains in an ecosystem. In reality, most organisms eat more than one thing and are eaten by more than one predator. Food webs capture this complexity. Understanding the difference matters because food chains can give the misleading impression that ecosystems are simple and linear, when they are actually complex networks. Disrupting one part of a food web can have unexpected ripple effects through many connected species.

docs.title: What is ecological succession?
docs.text: Ecological succession is the process by which the species composition of an ecosystem changes over time following a disturbance. Primary succession occurs on bare, lifeless surfaces (like cooled lava flows or retreating glaciers) where no soil exists. It starts with pioneer species like lichens and mosses that break down rock into soil. Secondary succession occurs after a disturbance (fire, flood, logging) that removes vegetation but leaves soil intact. It proceeds faster because soil, seeds, and root systems remain. Both types generally progress toward a more complex, stable community called a climax community, though ecologists now recognize that disturbances are a normal part of ecosystem dynamics and true "climax" states are rare.

docs.title: What is carrying capacity?
docs.text: Carrying capacity (K) is the maximum population size that an environment can sustain indefinitely given the available resources -- food, water, shelter, and space. When a population is small relative to K, resources are abundant and the population grows rapidly. As the population approaches K, resources become scarce, competition intensifies, disease spreads more easily, and the growth rate slows. In the logistic growth model, the population levels off at K. In reality, populations often overshoot their carrying capacity temporarily, leading to a crash when resources are depleted. Carrying capacity is not fixed; it changes with environmental conditions, technology, and resource availability. See Chapter 7 for population models.

docs.title: What is the difference between exponential and logistic population growth?
docs.text: Exponential growth occurs when a population increases at a constant rate without any resource limitations. The population grows faster and faster over time, producing a J-shaped curve. This happens when resources are unlimited, predators are absent, and disease is rare -- conditions that exist briefly when a species colonizes a new habitat. Logistic growth accounts for resource limitations. As the population approaches carrying capacity, growth slows and eventually levels off, producing an S-shaped (sigmoid) curve. In nature, exponential growth cannot continue indefinitely because Earth has finite resources. Understanding the difference between these models is essential for thinking about sustainability and the limits of growth.

docs.title: What are density-dependent and density-independent limiting factors?
docs.text: Density-dependent limiting factors are those whose effects increase as population density increases. Examples include competition for food and space, predation, disease, and parasitism. The more crowded a population becomes, the stronger these factors press against it. Density-independent limiting factors affect populations regardless of their size -- natural disasters (hurricanes, volcanic eruptions), extreme weather, and human activities (habitat destruction, pollution). Most real populations are regulated by a combination of both types. Understanding these factors helps explain why populations fluctuate and why some populations crash suddenly (density-independent event) while others decline gradually (density-dependent pressure).

docs.title: What is the tragedy of the commons?
docs.text: The tragedy of the commons describes a situation where individuals acting in their own self-interest deplete a shared resource, even though it is not in anyone's long-term interest. The concept comes from shared grazing land ("the commons") where each herder adds more cattle because the benefit goes to the individual while the cost of overgrazing is shared by everyone. Modern examples include overfishing in international waters, groundwater depletion from shared aquifers, and carbon emissions into the shared atmosphere. The tragedy of the commons is a systems thinking concept because it shows how individual rational behavior can produce collectively irrational outcomes. Solutions include regulation, privatization, and community management agreements.

docs.title: What are ecosystem services?
docs.text: Ecosystem services are the benefits that humans receive from functioning ecosystems. They are organized into four categories. Provisioning services are products we obtain: food, fresh water, timber, fiber, and medicine. Regulating services are benefits from ecosystem processes: pollination, water purification, climate regulation, flood control, and disease regulation. Cultural services are nonmaterial benefits: recreation, tourism, aesthetic value, spiritual significance, and educational opportunities. Supporting services underpin all the others: nutrient cycling, soil formation, primary production, and water cycling. Putting economic value on ecosystem services helps policymakers understand the true cost of environmental destruction -- when a wetland is drained, we lose water filtration, flood control, and habitat that would cost billions to replace with engineered systems.

docs.title: What is island biogeography?
docs.text: Island biogeography is a theory developed by Robert MacArthur and E.O. Wilson that explains how the number of species on an island is determined by two competing rates: immigration (new species arriving) and extinction (existing species dying out). Larger islands support more species because they have more habitats and larger populations less prone to extinction. Islands closer to the mainland receive more immigrants. The theory reaches an equilibrium species number where immigration and extinction rates balance. This theory extends beyond literal islands -- any isolated habitat patch (a forest fragment surrounded by farmland, a lake, a mountaintop) behaves like an island. This is why habitat fragmentation is so damaging to biodiversity.

docs.title: What are the major types of biomes?
docs.text: Biomes are classified into terrestrial and aquatic categories. Major terrestrial biomes include tropical rainforest (warm, wet, extremely biodiverse), temperate forest (moderate climate, deciduous or mixed trees), desert (very low precipitation, extreme temperatures), tundra (cold, permafrost, low-growing plants), grassland (moderate precipitation, dominated by grasses), chaparral (Mediterranean climate, fire-adapted shrubs), and taiga/boreal forest (cold, coniferous trees). Aquatic biomes include freshwater systems (streams, rivers, lakes, wetlands), estuaries (where freshwater meets saltwater), and marine systems (open ocean, coral reefs, deep ocean). Each biome is defined primarily by its climate, which determines what organisms can survive there. See Chapter 3 for detailed coverage.

docs.title: What is the nitrogen cycle?
docs.text: The nitrogen cycle traces how nitrogen moves between the atmosphere, soil, water, and living organisms. Although the atmosphere is 78% nitrogen gas (N2), most organisms cannot use it in that form. Nitrogen fixation -- performed by certain bacteria and lightning -- converts N2 into ammonia (NH3), which plants can absorb. Nitrification converts ammonia to nitrites and then nitrates, forms that plants use most easily. Assimilation is when plants incorporate nitrogen into proteins and nucleic acids. Ammonification returns nitrogen to the soil when decomposers break down dead organic matter. Denitrification converts nitrates back to N2 gas, completing the cycle. Human disruption of this cycle through synthetic fertilizers and fossil fuel combustion has doubled the amount of reactive nitrogen in the environment, causing eutrophication and dead zones.

docs.title: What is the phosphorus cycle?
docs.text: The phosphorus cycle is unique among the major biogeochemical cycles because it does not have a significant atmospheric component. Phosphorus moves between rocks, soil, water, and living organisms. It begins with the weathering of phosphorus-containing rocks, which releases phosphate ions into the soil. Plants absorb phosphate through their roots, and animals obtain it by eating plants. When organisms die, decomposers return phosphorus to the soil. Some phosphorus washes into rivers and eventually reaches the ocean, where it may be incorporated into marine sediments and, over millions of years, uplifted back into rock through geological processes. This cycle is extremely slow, which is why phosphorus is often a limiting nutrient in ecosystems and why phosphorus pollution from fertilizer runoff causes such severe eutrophication problems.

docs.title: What is the water (hydrologic) cycle?
docs.text: The water cycle describes how water moves continuously between the atmosphere, land surface, and underground reservoirs. Key processes include evaporation (water turns to vapor from surfaces and oceans), transpiration (plants release water vapor through their leaves), condensation (water vapor forms clouds), precipitation (rain, snow, sleet, hail), infiltration (water soaks into soil and becomes groundwater), and runoff (water flows over land into streams and rivers). The water cycle is powered by solar energy and gravity. Human activities alter this cycle through dam construction, groundwater pumping, deforestation (which reduces transpiration), and creating impervious surfaces (roads and buildings) that increase runoff and reduce infiltration. Understanding the water cycle is essential for managing freshwater resources sustainably.

docs.title: What is primary productivity?
docs.text: Primary productivity measures the rate at which producers (mostly plants and algae) convert sunlight into chemical energy through photosynthesis. Gross primary productivity (GPP) is the total amount of energy captured by photosynthesis. Net primary productivity (NPP) is the energy remaining after the producers use some for their own cellular respiration: NPP = GPP - Respiration. NPP represents the energy actually available to consumers in the ecosystem. Primary productivity varies enormously across biomes -- tropical rainforests and estuaries have the highest NPP per unit area, while deserts and open oceans have the lowest. NPP is important because it sets the energy budget for the entire ecosystem; every consumer depends on the energy that producers capture.

docs.title: What is eutrophication?
docs.text: Eutrophication is the process by which a body of water becomes enriched with excess nutrients, primarily nitrogen and phosphorus, leading to explosive growth of algae (an algal bloom). When the algae die, decomposer bacteria consume them and use up dissolved oxygen in the process, creating hypoxic (low oxygen) or anoxic (no oxygen) conditions called dead zones. Fish, shellfish, and other aquatic organisms suffocate and die. The main cause of cultural eutrophication (human-caused) is runoff from agricultural fertilizers, sewage, and animal waste. Famous examples include the dead zone in the Gulf of Mexico and algal blooms in Lake Erie. Eutrophication demonstrates how disrupting nutrient cycles cascades through entire aquatic ecosystems.

docs.title: What is the greenhouse effect?
docs.text: The greenhouse effect is a natural process that warms Earth's surface. When sunlight reaches Earth, some energy is absorbed by the surface and re-radiated as infrared radiation (heat). Greenhouse gases in the atmosphere -- including carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4), nitrous oxide (N2O), and water vapor -- absorb some of this outgoing heat and re-radiate it back toward the surface, keeping Earth about 33 degrees Celsius warmer than it would otherwise be. Without the greenhouse effect, Earth would be a frozen, lifeless planet. The problem is that human activities (burning fossil fuels, deforestation, agriculture) have increased greenhouse gas concentrations, enhancing the greenhouse effect and causing global warming. The greenhouse effect itself is not harmful -- it is the enhanced greenhouse effect that drives climate change.

docs.title: What is the difference between weather and climate?
docs.text: Weather refers to short-term atmospheric conditions in a specific place -- today's temperature, humidity, wind, and precipitation. Climate is the long-term pattern of weather in a region, typically averaged over 30 years or more. A cold day in January does not disprove global warming, just as a hot day in July does not prove it. Climate change refers to long-term shifts in average temperature, precipitation patterns, and extreme weather frequency across the entire planet. Confusing weather and climate is one of the most common misconceptions in environmental discussions. When scientists say the climate is warming, they mean the long-term global average is increasing -- not that every day will be hotter than the one before.

docs.title: What is the ozone layer and why is it important?
docs.text: The ozone layer is a region of the stratosphere (about 15 to 35 kilometers above Earth's surface) containing a relatively high concentration of ozone (O3) molecules. It absorbs 97 to 99 percent of the sun's ultraviolet (UV) radiation, protecting living organisms from DNA damage, skin cancer, cataracts, and harm to marine phytoplankton. In the 1980s, scientists discovered that chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) used in refrigerants and aerosol cans were destroying ozone molecules, creating a "hole" over Antarctica. The Montreal Protocol (1987) banned most CFCs, and the ozone layer has been slowly recovering -- one of the greatest success stories of international environmental policy. See Chapter 15 for the full story.

docs.title: What is the HIPPO framework?
docs.text: HIPPO is an acronym summarizing the five major threats to biodiversity: Habitat loss, Invasive species, Population growth (human), Pollution, and Overexploitation. Habitat loss (deforestation, urbanization, agriculture) is the single biggest driver of species extinction. Invasive species outcompete, prey on, or bring disease to native species. Human population growth increases demand for land and resources. Pollution degrades habitats through chemicals, plastics, and excess nutrients. Overexploitation means harvesting species faster than they can reproduce (overfishing, poaching, overhunting). The HIPPO framework is a useful memory tool for organizing your thinking about why biodiversity is declining globally. These five threats often interact and amplify each other.

docs.title: What are feedback loops in ecology?
docs.text: A feedback loop occurs when the output of a process feeds back to influence the same process. Reinforcing (positive) feedback loops amplify change -- they push a system further in the direction it is already moving. Example: as Arctic ice melts, the darker ocean surface absorbs more sunlight, which causes more warming, which melts more ice. Balancing (negative) feedback loops stabilize a system by counteracting change. Example: as a predator population grows, it depletes its prey, which reduces food availability, which slows predator population growth. Ecosystems are maintained by networks of balancing feedback loops, but when reinforcing feedback loops dominate, systems can change rapidly and reach tipping points. Understanding feedback loops is central to systems thinking. See Chapter 16 for more.

docs.title: What is sustainability?
docs.text: Sustainability means meeting the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. In ecological terms, it means using resources at a rate that does not exceed the environment's capacity to replenish them. A fishery is sustainable if the harvest rate does not exceed the reproduction rate. An agricultural system is sustainable if it maintains soil fertility, water quality, and biodiversity over the long term. Sustainability is one of the four Big Ideas in this course because nearly every environmental problem can be framed as a sustainability question: Are we using this resource faster than it can be renewed? What are the long-term consequences of current practices? What changes would bring our consumption in line with ecological limits?

docs.title: What are trophic levels?
docs.text: Trophic levels are the positions that organisms occupy in a food chain based on their energy source. Producers (plants, algae) are the first trophic level -- they convert sunlight into chemical energy. Primary consumers (herbivores) are the second level. Secondary consumers (carnivores that eat herbivores) are the third level. Tertiary consumers (top predators) are the fourth level. Decomposers break down dead material at all levels and return nutrients to the soil. Energy decreases at each successive trophic level (roughly 10% is passed on), which is why ecosystems support fewer organisms at higher trophic levels. This energy pyramid structure is a fundamental organizing principle in ecology.

docs.title: How do primary and secondary succession differ in detail?
docs.text: Ecological succession is the gradual, somewhat predictable process of change in species composition over time. Primary succession starts from scratch on bare, lifeless surfaces where no soil exists -- newly cooled lava, exposed rock from a retreating glacier, or a newly formed island. Pioneer species (lichens, mosses) colonize first and begin building soil. This process takes hundreds to thousands of years. Secondary succession occurs after a disturbance removes most vegetation but leaves soil intact -- after a wildfire, flood, clear-cut, or abandoned farmland. Because soil and seed banks remain, secondary succession is much faster, often reaching a mature forest stage within 100 to 200 years. Both types illustrate how ecosystems are dynamic, not static.

docs.title: What are the major types of species interactions?
docs.text: Species interactions are the ways organisms affect each other within a community. Major types include competition (two species competing for the same limited resource, harming both), predation (one species kills and eats another), mutualism (both species benefit, like bees and flowers), commensalism (one species benefits while the other is unaffected, like barnacles on a whale), and parasitism (one species benefits at the expense of the other, like ticks on a deer). These interactions shape community structure, drive natural selection, and create the complex food webs that sustain ecosystems. Understanding species interactions helps explain why removing or adding a single species can have far-reaching effects throughout an ecosystem.

docs.title: What is an ecological footprint?
docs.text: An ecological footprint measures the amount of biologically productive land and water area required to produce the resources a person consumes and absorb the waste they generate. It is expressed in global hectares. If everyone on Earth lived like the average American, we would need roughly five Earths to sustain that consumption. Ecological footprints allow us to compare the environmental impact of different lifestyles, diets, and nations. They make the abstract concept of sustainability concrete: if your footprint exceeds the biocapacity available per person on Earth (about 1.6 global hectares), your lifestyle is ecologically unsustainable at a global scale. Calculating and comparing ecological footprints is a powerful Apply-level skill covered in this course.

docs.title: How have human activities altered the major biogeochemical cycles?
docs.text: Human activities have dramatically altered all four major biogeochemical cycles. We have increased atmospheric CO2 by roughly 50% by burning fossil fuels and deforesting land (carbon cycle). We have more than doubled the amount of reactive nitrogen in the environment through synthetic fertilizer production and fossil fuel combustion (nitrogen cycle). We have accelerated phosphorus release through mining phosphate rock for fertilizer (phosphorus cycle). We have altered water flow patterns through dams, irrigation, groundwater pumping, and impervious surfaces (water cycle). These disruptions do not happen in isolation -- they interact. Excess nitrogen and phosphorus cause eutrophication in waterways. Excess CO2 causes ocean acidification and climate change. Understanding these connections is the essence of systems thinking in ecology.

docs.title: What is the difference between r-selected and K-selected species?
docs.text: r-selected species are adapted for rapid reproduction and colonization of unstable environments. They produce many offspring with little parental care, mature quickly, and have short lifespans. Examples include insects, bacteria, mice, and many weeds. K-selected species are adapted for stable environments near carrying capacity. They produce few offspring with extensive parental care, mature slowly, and have long lifespans. Examples include elephants, whales, humans, and old-growth trees. The "r" refers to the intrinsic rate of increase (reproduction speed), while "K" refers to carrying capacity. Most species fall somewhere on a continuum between these extremes rather than at one end or the other. See Chapter 7 for more on reproductive strategies.

docs.title: What is the Rule of 70 and how do I use it?
docs.text: The Rule of 70 is a quick calculation for estimating how long it takes a population (or any quantity growing at a constant percentage rate) to double. The formula is: Doubling Time = 70 / Growth Rate (as a percentage). If a population is growing at 2% per year, it will double in 70 / 2 = 35 years. If it is growing at 0.5% per year, doubling time is 70 / 0.5 = 140 years. This rule works because of the mathematics of exponential growth (it is derived from the natural logarithm of 2, which is approximately 0.693, rounded to 0.70 for easy mental math). The Rule of 70 is useful for quickly grasping the implications of growth rates -- a seemingly small 3% annual growth rate means doubling in just 23 years.

docs.title: What is the difference between GPP and NPP?
docs.text: Gross Primary Productivity (GPP) is the total amount of energy (or carbon) that producers fix through photosynthesis over a given time period. Net Primary Productivity (NPP) is the energy remaining after producers use some for their own cellular respiration (R). The relationship is: NPP = GPP - R. Think of it this way: GPP is the producer's gross income, R is the cost of living, and NPP is the net income available to invest (in growth) or to be consumed by herbivores and decomposers. NPP is what matters for the rest of the food web because it represents the energy actually available to consumers. Typical units are grams of carbon per square meter per year (g C/m^2/yr) or kilocalories per square meter per year.

docs.title: What is the difference between point source and nonpoint source pollution?
docs.text: Point source pollution comes from a single, identifiable location -- a factory pipe discharging wastewater, a sewage treatment plant outfall, or a smokestack. It is relatively easy to monitor, regulate, and control. Nonpoint source pollution comes from many diffuse sources spread across a large area -- agricultural runoff carrying fertilizer and pesticides, urban stormwater picking up oil and trash from streets, or sediment eroding from construction sites. Nonpoint source pollution is much harder to control because there is no single pipe to regulate. In the United States, nonpoint source pollution (especially agricultural runoff) is now the leading cause of water quality problems, even though point sources are better regulated under the Clean Water Act.

docs.title: What is LD50 and how are dose-response curves interpreted?
docs.text: LD50 (Lethal Dose 50) is the dose of a substance that kills 50% of a test population, typically expressed as milligrams of substance per kilogram of body weight (mg/kg). A lower LD50 means a more toxic substance. For example, caffeine has an LD50 of about 192 mg/kg in rats, while botulinum toxin has an LD50 of about 0.001 mg/kg -- making botulinum toxin roughly 192,000 times more toxic. A dose-response curve plots the dose of a substance on the x-axis against the percentage of organisms showing a response (such as death) on the y-axis, typically producing an S-shaped curve. These curves help toxicologists determine safe exposure levels and identify threshold doses below which no adverse effects are observed.

docs.title: What are the three types of survivorship curves?
docs.text: Survivorship curves describe the pattern of mortality in a population over time. Type I shows high survival throughout most of life with most deaths occurring in old age. Humans and large mammals follow this pattern -- few offspring, extensive parental care. Type II shows a constant mortality rate throughout life, meaning an individual has the same chance of dying at any age. Many birds, some reptiles, and some rodents follow this pattern. Type III shows very high mortality early in life with the few survivors living a long time. Many fish, insects, plants, and marine invertebrates follow this pattern -- they produce enormous numbers of offspring, most of which die young. These curves relate directly to r-selected (Type III) and K-selected (Type I) strategies.

docs.title: What is the difference between renewable and nonrenewable resources?
docs.text: Renewable resources are naturally replenished on a human timescale -- solar energy, wind, geothermal heat, timber (if harvested sustainably), and freshwater (through the water cycle). Nonrenewable resources exist in finite quantities and are not replenished on human timescales -- fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas), nuclear fuel (uranium), and mineral ores. Some resources fall in a gray area: soil is technically renewable but takes thousands of years to form, so erosion can make it effectively nonrenewable. Groundwater in deep aquifers may take thousands of years to recharge. The distinction matters for sustainability -- relying on nonrenewable resources means they will eventually run out, while renewable resources can theoretically be used indefinitely if managed properly.
